coredata.dc:title: A Y-shape-structured electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on carbon quantum dots and locked nucleic acid probe for microRNA determination with single-base resolution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Yu
Huang, Lin-Xiao
Lin, Mu-Tu
Zhang, Zi-Yang
Liu, Ai-Lin
Lei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Since microRNAs (miRNAs) are predictors of tumorigenesis, accurate identification and quantification of miRNAs with highly similar sequences are expected to reflect tumor diagnosis and treatment. In this study, a highly selective and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was constructed for miRNAs determination based on Y-shaped junction structure equipped with locked nucleic acids (LNA), graphene oxide-based nanocomposite to enrich luminophores, and conductive matrix. Specifically, two LNA-modified probes were designed for specific miRNA recognition, that is, a dual-amine functionalized hairpin capture probe and a signal probe. A Y-shaped DNA junction structure was generated on the electrode surface upon miRNA hybridizing across the two branches, so as to enhance the selectivity. Carbon quantum dots-polyethylene imine-graphene oxide (CQDs-PEI-GO) nanocomposites were developed to enrich luminophores CQDs, and thus enhancing the ECL intensity. For indirect signal amplification, an electrochemically activated poly(2-aminoterephthalic acid) (ATA) film decorated with gold nanoparticles was prepared on electrode as an effective matrix to accelerate the electron transfer. The fabricated ECL biosensor achieved sensitive determination of miRNA-222 with a limit-of-detection (LOD) as low as 1.95 fM (S/N = 3). Notably, Y-shaped junction structures equipped with LNA probes endowed ECL biosensor with salient single-base discrimination ability and anti-interference capacity. Overall, the proposed Y-shaped ECL biosensor has considerable promise for clinical biomarker determination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Y-shaped junction structure
Locked nucleic acid
CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposites
2-Aminoterephthalic acid
miRNA-222

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Design of the ECL biosensor coupled Y-shaped junction structure
Characterization of CQDs, GO and CQDs-PEI-GO
Morphological and elemental characterization of AuNPs/ATA/GCE
EIS characterization of the ECL biosensor
ECL and CV characterization of the ECL biosensor
Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor
Specificity, anti-interference and reproducibility of the ECL biosensor
Application of the ECL biosensor in serum samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province ( 2016Y9055 and 2019Y9005 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China ( 2019J01301 and 2023J01551 ), Social Development Guiding Programs of Fujian Province of China ( 2019Y0012 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of CQDs. TEM images of GO (C) and CQDs-PEI-GO (D). XPS survey (E), high-resolution C1s (F) and O1s (G) XPS spectra of CQDs. (H) Normalized fluorescence spectrum of CQDS in PBS and ECL spectrum of CQDs in PBS containing 100 mM K2S2O8 and 60 mM H2O2. Inset of A: Size distribution of CQDs. Inset of B: Lattice fringe image of CQDs. Inset of H: The photographs of the blank H2O (left) and the CQDs solution (right) under daylight lamp (upper) and under UV light (304 nm, bottom).

SEM images of bare GCE (A) and AuNPs/ATA/GCE (B). (C) EDS spectrum of AuNPs/ATA/GCE. (D) EIS of bare GCE (a), ATA/GCE before (b) and after (c) electrochemical activation, AuNPs/ATA/GCE (d), ssDNA/AuNPs/ATA/GCE (e), ssDNA/MCH/AuNPs/ATA/GCE (f) and dsDNA/AuNPs/ATA/GCE (g). Inset of E: An enlarged EIS of curves a, c and d. (E) ECL spectra and (F) CV curves of bare GCE (a), ATA/GCE after electrochemical activation (b), AuNPs/ATA/GCE (c), ssDNA/AuNPs/ATA/GCE (d) and dsDNA/AuNPs/ATA/GCE (e).

(A) ECL signals of miRNA-222 at different concentrations and (B) the linear relationship between △I ECL and miRNA-222 concentrations. curve a to i: 0, 0.01, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5 and 50 pM miRNA-222. (C) Specificity of the Y-shaped ECL biosensor using LNA probes and DNA probes toward fully complementary miRNA-222 (a), single-base mismatched nucleotide (b), three-base mismatched nucleotide (c), five-base mismatched nucleotide (d), fully-mismatched nucleotide (e) and the blank group (f). (D) Selectivity of the Y-shaped ECL biosensor toward miRNA-222 (a), miRNA-21 (b), miRNA-126 (c), let7a (d), fully-mismatched-RNA (e) and blank (f).
Schematic illustration of the Y-shaped ECL biosensor for the determination of miRNA-222.
ECL measurement of miRNA-222 in human serum (n = 3).
Comparison of different methods for miRNA-222 detection.

row.entry: Chromogenic reaction-free distance-based microfluidic paper-based analytical devices
CQDs and Y-shaped junction structure equipped with LNA

head.ce:title: A Y-shape-structured electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on carbon quantum dots and locked nucleic acid probe for microRNA determination with single-base resolution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yu Zhong and Lin-Xiao Huang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since microRNAs (miRNAs) are predictors of tumorigenesis, accurate identification and quantification of miRNAs with highly similar sequences are expected to reflect tumor diagnosis and treatment. In this study, a highly selective and sensitive electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor was constructed for miRNAs determination based on Y-shaped junction structure equipped with locked nucleic acids (LNA), graphene oxide-based nanocomposite to enrich luminophores, and conductive matrix. Specifically, two LNA-modified probes were designed for specific miRNA recognition, that is, a dual-amine functionalized hairpin capture probe and a signal probe. A Y-shaped DNA junction structure was generated on the electrode surface upon miRNA hybridizing across the two branches, so as to enhance the selectivity. Carbon quantum dots-polyethylene imine-graphene oxide (CQDs-PEI-GO) nanocomposites were developed to enrich luminophores CQDs, and thus enhancing the ECL intensity. For indirect signal amplification, an electrochemically activated poly(2-aminoterephthalic acid) (ATA) film decorated with gold nanoparticles was prepared on electrode as an effective matrix to accelerate the electron transfer. The fabricated ECL biosensor achieved sensitive determination of miRNA-222 with a limit-of-detection (LOD) as low as 1.95 fM (S/N = 3). Notably, Y-shaped junction structures equipped with LNA probes endowed ECL biosensor with salient single-base discrimination ability and anti-interference capacity. Overall, the proposed Y-shaped ECL biosensor has considerable promise for clinical biomarker determination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs), a class of single-strand, short and endogenous noncoding RNAs with ca. 19–24 nucleotides, are gradually stressed and applied as an adjunct or alternative in early diagnosis and long-term monitoring of disease (Cheong et al., 2022). Regrettably, generally limited in their small size, ultralow expression, high homology and susceptibility to degradation, miRNAs detection approach with required sensitivity and specificity remains a challenge (Wang et al., 2020; Cai et al., 2021). To date, various techniques have been explored for miRNA quantification (Ning et al., 2021; Hwang et al., 2021; Weng et al., 2022), including northern blotting, reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR), microarrays analysis, electrochemistry, electrochemiluminescence (ECL), fluorescence, surface plasmon resonance (SPR) and surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS). As a combination of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence, ECL has attracted great attention for constructing miRNA sensing platform due to the outstanding properties of high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, low background signal and rapid response (Ning et al., 2021; Zhao et al., 2022).

The selectivity between DNA probes and target sequence is a key point for the ECL biosensor for miRNA analysis. The application of Y-shaped DNA junction structure is an effective approach to improve selectivity. It is basically constructed with three complementary oligonucleotide branches, that is, two DNA probes and target sequence. Without target sequence, capture and signal probes can not efficiently hybridize due to low melting temperature (T m) (Murakami et al., 2012). Only in the presence of target DNA, the target binding induces the proximity effect of the two motifs, so as to form Y-shaped junction structure. The formed nanostructures serve as a vital building block for specific and sensitive signal readout toward various analytes, such as ion (Ma et al., 2019), nucleic acid (Zhou et al., 2023; Chang et al., 2021), protein (Li et al., 2013), cell (Li et al., 2020), etc. Previously, the ternary Y-shaped DNA junction was widely used in homogeneous phase determination, such as fluorescent sensing platform. For example, Nakayama et al. (2008) developed a ternary junction probe with a strong single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) identifying capability. Zhu et al. (2014) introduced a label-free fluorescent method based on DNA three-way junction driven-strand displacement mode for DNA strand detection. Currently, there are different kinds of Y-shaped junction structures developed to meet the requirement of biosensor. For instance, our group (Wang et al., 2015) designed single-thiol modified capture probes separately assembled onto gold electrodes for formation of the Y-shaped junction structures by hybridization, allowing detection of acute promyelocytic leukemia related gene down to a detection limit of 47 fM. However, the long-standing hurdles still remain in the heterogeneous surface-confined capture probe involving the control of orientation, distribution and packing density. To solve aforementioned defects, Li et al. (2016) reported a dual-thiol functionalized hairpin DNA probe (dSH-HSP) as capture probe to assemble on electrode, keep proper upright orientation conformation and form a Y-shaped DNA junction upon target binding. Compared with single-thiol modified hairpin DNA probe, dSH-HSP showed higher molecular rigidity, more precise control, lower detection limit, better one-base mismatch discrimination ability and higher reproducibility. However, accurately identifying miRNAs with highly homologous sequences, particularly those with one-base mismatch in biological samples, is still a huge challenge. In this regard, to further enhance the selectivity of Y-shaped structure biosensor, the introduction of locked nucleic acid (LNA, a high affinity oligomer) into Y-shaped junction structure can largely augment the oligonucleotide binding affinity, effectively improve the recognition ability, greatly stabilize the hybrid duplex, obviously suppress background signal and enhance thermal stability (its T m was nearly 10 °C higher than normal DNA) (You et al., 2006; Lei et al., 2013; Ustuner et al., 2021). For example, our group reported that the current signal difference obtained by hybridization of LNA probe with complementary sequence and single-base mismatched sequence was 2 times larger than that obtained by DNA probe (Wang et al., 2011). Ustuner et al. (2021) adopted the LNA modified magnetic beads to specifically concentrate the target miRNA before electrochemical detection for a large pool of oligonucleotides with negligible interferences. Given together, ECL biosensors equipped with Y-shaped DNA junction structures involving hairpin capture probes and signal probes with LNA modification are conducive for selective determination of miRNA, and to our knowledge, there is limited literature to document.

Further, concerning the extremely low miRNA expression, the ECL biosensors need to be integrated with nanomaterials with high luminescence efficiency and large quantities, so as to meet the sensitivity required (Xu et al., 2018). Currently, ECL luminophores are mainly composed of Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminol, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and their analogues. However, the fundamental limitation of these luminophores is the lack of functional groups on Ru(bpy)3 2+, weak signal generation of luminol in physiologically neutral solution and toxicity of semiconductor QDs (Cui et al., 2004; Filali et al., 2020). Correspondingly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial, have been widely employed in ECL biosensor attributed to its abundant surface groups, high electrical conductivity and good biocompatibility (Rasal et al., 2021; Feng et al., 2022). Furthermore, there are many proposals to improve the ECL intensity via increasing the number of excited ECL luminophores.

Graphene oxide (GO) is deriving from graphene and inheriting the merits of large surface area and good mechanical strength, even possessing more oxygen functional groups. As previously reported (Zhang et al., 2021), GO was capable of immobilizing a mass of CQDs owing to its single layer honeycomb structure, and the fabricated CQDs-GO nanocomposite was able to load substantial DNA, so as to enhance the sensitivity of biosensor. However, GO is encumbered by easily forming agglomerates due to high cohesive van der Waals energy (Li et al., 2017). In this regard, polyethylene imine (PEI), when functionalized with GO via electrostatic adsorption, can improve monodispersity of GO and endow the nanocomposite with amino group to efficiently bind with CQDs with carboxyl groups via amide bond. Hence, CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposite is beneficial to subsequent step for enriching ECL luminophores and labeling signal probes.

In this work, given the defects of low content and high homology of miRNA, an ECL biosensor coupled with a triple signal amplification strategy based on Y-shaped junction structure prepared with LNA probes, CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposites and conductive matrix was developed for miRNAs determination with salient single-base resolution and sensitivity. As a proof-of-concept, miRNA-222, a transcriptionally up-regulated gene in patients with papillary thyroid cancer (PTC), was used in this study, which has been considered as a reliable biomarker for early diagnosis and long-running test of PTC. As shown in Scheme 1 , a polymer film of poly(2-amino-terephthalic acid) (ATA) was firstly electropolymerized and activated on glassy carbon electrode (GCE). Of note, the electrochemically activating ATA film with K2S2O8 and H2O2 can greatly reduce impedance and enhance electron-transfer ability, so as to achieve indirect signal amplification. Then gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were successively loaded on its surface (AuNPs/ATA/GCE) to further decrease impedance and increase ECL intensity. The dual-NH2 modified hairpin capture DNA probe, acting as a rigid scaffold to keep stem upright and form the Y-shaped junction structure on the electrode surface upon target binding, was immobilized on the AuNPs/ATA/GCE surface via dual Au–N bonds. Correspondingly, both capture probe and signal probe were modified with LNA to reduce the conformational flexibility of ribose and enhance the selectivity for complementary sequences. Upon target miRNA binding, the Y-shaped junction structures were efficiently formed and ECL signals from CQDs-PEI-GO labeled with signal DNA were obtained. Overall, combining the advantages of the electrochemically activating AuNPs-ATA substrate and CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposite, the ECL signal of CQDs was greatly enhanced and a rigid Y-shaped structure ECL biosensor was established for the determination of PTC-related miRNA-222 with high sensitivity and single-base mismatch discrimination capacity.
The reagents, apparatus and methods are provided in the Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the ECL biosensor coupled Y-shaped junction structure
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the high sequence homology among miRNA families in complex biological sample determination, DNA probes with good specificity and high hybridization efficiency need to be designed and synthesized. In this study, based on the sequence of target miRNA-222, a group of DNA probes were designed (listed in Table S1), including a hairpin capture probe with two amino groups at 5′- and 3′-ends and a LNA location, and a signal probe with an amino group at 3′-end and two LNA locations. Importantly, the presence of LNA in capture and signal probes was designed, so as to reduce non-specific binding and further weaken the background signal. The dual-amino modified capture probe could robustly be coupled onto AuNPs/ATA/GCE surface via dual Au–N bonds. The stem of capture probe was composed of 5 base pairs (bp), serving as a rigid support to keep the probe upright. And the loop of capture probe provided a platform to partially hybridize with miRNA-222 (8 bp) and signal probe (7 bp), respectively. Before target binding, the signal probe could not bind to the electrode surface because the melting temperature (T m, 5.7 °C, calculated by bioinformatics software at https://sg.idtdna.com/calc/analyzer) was considerably below the operating temperature (37 °C). However, upon miRNA-222 introduction, the Y-shaped junction structure was formed, because the T m (64.9 °C) was above the operating temperature. Meanwhile, the binding interaction among target, signal and the capture probes was assessed via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) measurements. In Table S2, the association constant (K a) among target/signal/capture probes was ca. 5.8 × 105 M−1 (Fig. S1B). However, the binding affinity between signal and capture probes was obviously reduced in Fig. S1A (K a = 1.0 × 103 M−1). Hence, the hybridization among capture/signal/target sequences possessed high binding affinity and the “Y”-shaped junction structure could be stably formed, which was consistent with literature (Li et al., 2016). Subsequently, CQDs-PEI-GO was used as labeling agent to tag signal probes via amido conjugation. Thereinto, GO possesses excellent mechanical properties, large surface area and favorable biocompatibility (Guo et al., 2022), serving as a support material. The functionalization of PEI on GO not only supplies good solubility, but provides amino groups to immobilize CQDs (Li et al., 2017; Qin et al., 2019). The significant amplification of ECL response from luminophores CQDs can be generated with the hybridization among capture probe, signal probe and target sequence. On the contrary, if the stable Y-shaped junction structure was not formed, CQDs were easily detached from electrode surface, resulting in weak ECL signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs, GO and CQDs-PEI-GO
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of CQDs, GO and CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposite was characterized with transmission electron microscope (TEM). The synthesized CQDs exhibited uniform spherical shapes with the average size of ca. 2.89 nm (Fig. 1 A). High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images showed the high crystallinity of the CQDs, where the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm (Fig. 1B), corresponding to the (100) direction d-spacing of graphite carbon (Hong and Yang, 2022). TEM image of GO (Fig. 1C) revealed the pleated structures with yarn-like ripples. After the formation of CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposite, it could be seen that the wrinkle structures of GO disappeared and a large number of small black spots adhered to the thin layer (Fig. 1D), illustrating that CQDs had been successfully immobilized on PEI-GO surface. To further explore the surface functional properties of CQDs, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum and X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of CQDs were obtained. In Fig. S2, a broad absorption band at 3380 cm−1 was observed due to the O–H stretching vibration. The adsorption peaks also exhibited the carbonyl group (−CO) stretching vibration of carboxyl (−COOH) at 1850 and 1640 cm−1, the hydroxyl (−OH) in-plane bending vibration of carboxyl at 1380 cm−1, the C–O stretching vibration at 1090 cm−1 and the –OH out-of-plane bending vibration of carboxyl at 885 cm−1. The FTIR spectrum indicated the abundant oxygen-containing groups on CQDs surface. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra and fluorescence spectra of CQDs were displayed in Fig. S3. Additionally, XPS spectrum (Fig. 1E) exhibited three elements including oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and carbon (C) of CQDs. In detail, XPS spectra (Fig. 1F and G) showed the absorption peaks of C1s at 284.9 eV (CC), 285.9 eV (C–O), 287.0 eV (CO) and 288.3 eV (O–CO), and the absorption peaks of O1s at 531.2 eV (CO), 532.8 eV (C–O) and 533.5 eV (CO/COOH), which were consistent with FTIR result. These results indicated the ample carboxyl groups on CQDs, which is conducive to react with NH2-functionalized PEI-GO through amido-bond to form CQDs-PEI-GO nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and elemental characterization of AuNPs/ATA/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of GCE were observed before and after AuNPs/ATA modification. As displayed in Fig. 2 A, the surface of bare GCE was smooth, and there was almost no residual impurity. After electropolymerization of ATA and electrodeposition of AuNPs, Fig. 2B exhibited that AuNPs with uniform particle size were well distributed on ATA/GCE surface. Furthermore, the elemental composition of AuNPs/ATA/GCE was characterized with energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). It could be seen from Fig. 2C that the surface of the electrode contained C, N, O and Au elements. The morphological and elemental results indicated the successful preparation of AuNPs/ATA/GCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: EIS characterization of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical characteristics of electrodes during the assembly process were investigated by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in 10 mM K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] solution containing 100 mM KCl (1:1) as an electrochemical redox probe. As shown in Fig. 2D, bare GCE possessed the lowest electronic transfer resistance (R et, 88 Ω) value, indicating its excellent electron transfer capability (curve a). When ATA film was electropolymerized on the GCE surface, the greatly enhanced R et value (curve b, 1850 Ω) may be related to the electrostatic repulsion between the deprotonated carboxyl group of ATA and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. After cyclic voltammetry (CV) scanning at a negative potential in K2S2O8–H2O2 solution, the ATA film was electrically activated to improve its conductivity, and the R et value significantly reduced (curve c, 250 Ω), which is consistent with literature reported (Jin et al., 2018). Moreover, the R et value further decreased to 130 Ω (curve d) with addition of AuNPs due to the acceleration of the electron transfer process. Hereto, the AuNPs/ATA/GCE was successfully prepared. When AuNPs/ATA/GCE assembled with capture probe, R et value greatly enhanced (curve e, 1140 Ω) due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged phosphate backbone groups and negatively charged [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−, which greatly impeded the electron transfer. The unoccupied space of electrode was blocked by MCH, generating a certain steric hindrance and increasing the R et value (curve f, 1505 Ω). Subsequently, with the introduction of miRNA-222 and signal probe, the Y-shaped DNA structure was formed. With a number of negatively charged DNA phosphate skeleton groups on the electrode surface increased sharply, [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− further repelled, resulted in low electron transfer and increment of R et (curve g, 2425 Ω). The EIS result manifested the biosensor interface had been successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and CV characterization of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The ECL spectra and CV curves of the biosensor assembly process were shown in Fig. 2E and F, respectively. Bare GCE showed very low response in both ECL signal and current signal (curve a), which originated from the dual co-reactive agent (K2S2O8–H2O2). After the electrochemically activating ATA was covalently bonded on bare electrode, the ECL intensity increased slightly (curve b). When AuNPs deposited on ATA/GCE surface, both ECL signal and current signal increased owing to the excellent electrical conductivity of AuNPs (curve c). After the capture probe self-assembly on AuNPs/ATA/GCE, the electron transport was blocked due to the steric hindrance of DNA, and the ECL intensity and current intensity relatively reduced (curve d). When the hybridization reaction happened among capture probe, signal probe and target miRNA-222, the Y-shaped junction structures robustly formed. As ECL emitter, the assembly of CQDs loaded on the electrode surface resulted in significant signal enhancement (curve e). Consequently, the ECL and CV responses of different modified electrodes indicated the biosensor construction was feasible.

To investigate the ECL behavior between CQDs and double coreactants, the CV curves and ECL potential profiles of AuNPs/ATA/GCE scanning from 0 to −1.6 V in 10 mM PBS (pH 7.4) containing different substances were obtained (Fig. S4). It can be seen that there was no ECL signal produced when CV experiment was carried out for blank PBS (curve a). The CV curve of AuNPs/ATA/GCE in 100 mM K2S2O8 solution showed the reduction current increased while leveled off after −0.7 V (Fig. S4A, curve b), demonstrating SO4 •− formation by reduction of S2O8 2− (Han et al., 2019). At the same time, the corresponding ECL curve showed a slight ECL signal when the potential was negative than −0.7 V and peaked at −1.6 V (Fig. S4B, curve b). The CV curve of AuNPs/ATA/GCE in 60 mM H2O2 exhibited a noticeable reduction peak at −0.8 V, accompanied by a reduction of H2O2 to OH• (Fig. S4A, curve c). However, there was no obvious ECL signal. After the introduction of CQDs in PBS, the reduction current peak appeared at −0.7 V and the ECL signal slightly increased compared with blank PBS (Fig. S4, curve d). CV and ECL curves of K2S2O8 and H2O2 (curve e), K2S2O8 and CQDs (curve f), CQDs and H2O2 (curve g) were also recorded. It was found that the ECL signals of these three systems were enhanced compared with those of PBS containing K2S2O8, CQDs and H2O2 alone, especially for K2S2O8 and CQDs (Fig. S4B, curve f) in which the ECL signal climbed sharply with the strongest ECL signal appearing at −1.6 V. The observation could be attributed to the radiative deactivation of the excited state of CQDs (CQDs*) resulting from CQDs-K2S2O8 system. To verify the role of the dual coreactants, the CV and ECL profiles were examined for CQDs-K2S2O8–H2O2 system. The strongest ECL signal was obtained (Fig. S4B, curve h), which was 1.42 and 8.17 times greater than that of K2S2O8 or H2O2 as the coreactant. Consequently, the improved ECL intensity of CQDs via dual coreactants could be due to the increment of SO4 •− by H2O2 (Dai et al., 2015). The proposed strategy to enhance CQDs ECL emission based on the synergistic effect between H2O2 and K2S2O8 composed of the following processes. As the potential became enough negative, S2O8 2− was reduced to an oxidant SO4 •− (reaction 1). OH• could be formed from H2O2 (reaction 2), so SO4 •− formation proceeded (reaction 3). S2O8 •− reacted with the reduced species of CQDs (CQDs•−), resulting in production of the excited state of CQDs (CQDs*) to emit light. It was illustrated as follows:
(1) S2O8 2− + e− → SO4 •− + SO4 2−
(2) H2O2 + e− → OH• + OH−
(3) OH• + S2O8 2− → SO4 •− + HSO4 − + 1/2 O2
(4) CQDs + e− → CQDs•−
(5) SO4 •− + CQDs•− → CQDs* + SO4 2−
(6) CQDs* → CQDs + hv

Compared to the fluorescence spectrum with an emission peak at 400 nm, ECL spectrum of CQDs showed an emission maximum located at ca. 530 nm (Fig. 1H) and a red shift of 130 nm was observed, demonstrating the intrinsically different luminescence mechanisms.

In order to obtain the best sensing performance, the deposition time of AuNPs, hybridization time and temperature, the concentrations of H2O2 and K2S2O8 were optimized and showed in the Supplementary Material. In short, 100 s deposition time of AuNPs, 50 min hybridization time, 60 mM H2O2, 100 mM K2S2O8 and 37 °C of hybridization temperature were found to present the best results (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimized experimental conditions, the quantitative measurements of the obtained ECL biosensor were carried out by adding different concentrations of miRNA-222. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the elevated concentrations of miRNA-222 caused an increment in ECL signal. With the increment of miRNA-222 concentration, the number of Y-shaped junction structure was increased and the accumulated CQDs were attached to the electrode surface, so the ECL signal gradually enhanced. The difference (△I ECL) in the ECL intensity, that is, detection signal minus the background signal of K2S2O8 and H2O2, was adopted as the measurement signal. In Fig. 3B, the △I ECL value was linear with the logarithm value of miRNA-222 concentration over the range from 0.025 pM to 50 pM. The regression equation was △I ECL = 1643.11 + 564.04 × lgC (pM) with a correlation coefficient of 0.998. The detection limit for miRNA-222 was as low as 1.95 fM (S/N = 3), demonstrating the proposed ECL biosensor was able to sensitively determine miRNA-222.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, anti-interference and reproducibility of the ECL biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the ECL biosensor plays an important role in analyzing biological samples. As previously mentioned, LNA (a special bicyclic nucleotide derivative) selectively hybridizes with complementary RNA or DNA, possesses higher affinity than the corresponding DNA probe and improves the sequence specificity. Hence, we evaluated the specificity of LNA modified Y-shaped structure ECL biosensor via determining several kinds of nucleotide sequences (Table S1) including fully complementary miRNA-222, single-base mismatched nucleotide, three-base mismatched nucleotide, five-base mismatched nucleotide and fully mismatched nucleotide. For comparison, the same sensing mode of ECL using LNA probe and corresponding DNA probe was carried out, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3C, both LNA and DNA probes exhibited maximum ECL intensity for miRNA-222 (group a), in which the LNA probe produced slightly larger ECL response. When one-base mismatch occurred (group b), △I ECL generated from LNA probe manifested about 4.7 times smaller than that of miRNA-222. It's noteworthy that for DNA probe, the difference of △I ECL between fully complementary miRNA-222 and one-base mismatch was only 2.2 times. Based on the difference of △I ECL values, LNA probes showed much higher hybridization capability compared with the corresponding DNA probes. With the increment of mismatched base number, the △I ECL value became smaller until little differences from blank group (group c to group e), because less efficient hybridization occurred. PBS without target miRNA acted as a blank group was investigated under the same condition. The blank group displayed negligible ECL responses (group f) from little hybridization between capture probe and signal probe owing to the low T m (5.7 °C). In this regard, the combination of the LNA probes with the Y-shaped structure biosensor showed an excellent single-based discrimination ability, and its background interference resistance lays the foundation for practical application.

Meanwhile, anti-interference capacity is also an important indicator of biosensor performance. To assess the anti-interference ability of the biosensor, miRNA-126, miRNA-21, let7a and completely mismatched RNA were employed as interferents and respectively determined by the proposed biosensor. In Fig. 3D, as a control, the signal generated by the hybridization solution only containing signal probe and capture probe was small (group f), because of no Y-shaped DNA structure formation and the absence of CQDs on electrode surface. The ECL responses of various interferents (groups b-e) were as low as the blank group and far below the target group (group a), indicating the prepared biosensor had high anti-interference capacity toward miRNA-222 detection. Additionally, to evaluate the reproducibility of the proposed biosensor, the experiment was repeated 5 times with the same concentration of the target miRNA-222 under the same conditions, and the change value of each ECL signal was observed. The relative standard deviation (RSD) of five biosensors was 4.42% (data not shown), displaying its merit of favorable reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the ECL biosensor in serum samples

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a highly selective and sensitive ECL biosensor coupled with triply amplified strategy, involving the employment of Y-shaped DNA junction structures prepared with LNA probes, the electrochemically activating substrate and nanocomposite, was successfully developed for PTC-related miRNA-222 determination. Explicitly, a Y-shaped junction structure equipped with LNA probes demonstrated high molecular rigidity, salient one-base mismatch discrimination ability and excellent anti-interference capacity. Additionally, AuNPs/ATA film was able to lower impedance, promote electron transfer rate and improve reductive-oxidation ECL. When PEI-GO functionalized with CQDs, the nanocomposite as luminophores could realize signal probe labeling and enrichment, so as to enhance ECL intensity. Taking virtues of aforementioned materials, the prepared ECL biosensor was capable of quantitatively determining miRNA-222 with a LOD as low as 1.95 fM (S/N = 3) and achieving in serum sample analysis. Hence, the proposed ECL biosensor provides a promising potential for early clinical diagnosis and long-running monitoring of disease-related biomarkers.

Yu Zhong: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Lin-Xiao Huang: Investigation, Writing. Mu-Tu Lin: Investigation. Zi-Yang Zhang: Writing. Ai-Lin Liu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Yun Lei: Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2016Y9055 and 2019Y9005), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province of China (2019J01301 and 2023J01551), Social Development Guiding Programs of Fujian Province of China (2019Y0012).
